lines.text: - KODA Office, final consultation -
A small conference room is packed with chairs.
Coffee, Milo, and biscuits sit on one side of the table. In Fiji, meetings begin this way.
Vikash from National Planning sets down his briefcase and pours a cup of Milo.
Sela from the Land Office takes the seat beside him. James from APAT opens the door and enters.
Ratu Josefa and Lani have come up from Naitamba. Mere guides people to their seats.
For the first time, everyone involved in this project is in one room.

lines.speaker: Mere
lines.text: Bula, there's coffee. Milo too. Let's talk while we drink.
A brief tea time. The awkward air begins to loosen.

lines.speaker: Mere
lines.text: I'll start with the field talanoa and findings.

lines.speaker: Mere
lines.text: The main problems with Naitamba's existing water system are aging pipes and damaged storage tanks.

lines.speaker: Mere
lines.text: The water source itself is stable. If we replace infrastructure and build a maintenance system, it can work.

lines.speaker: Mere
lines.text: The key is who manages it after installation. If the vanua is not the owner, the result will be the same as ten years ago.

lines.speaker: Vikash
lines.text: Namaste. I will share National Planning's review result.

lines.speaker: Vikash
lines.text: A project plan that includes a village-based management model can be approved.

lines.speaker: Vikash
lines.text: I'm glad the direction discussed in our previous meeting was reflected.

lines.speaker: Vikash
lines.text: The documents have arrived, but...

lines.speaker: Vikash
lines.text: The village management plan is still weak. Supplementary material is needed.

lines.speaker: Vikash
lines.text: At this stage, I can only recommend conditional approval.

lines.speaker: Vikash
lines.text: To be frank -

lines.speaker: Vikash
lines.text: I don't see how this plan is different from ten years ago.

lines.speaker: Vikash
lines.text: Approval within the deadline will be difficult.

lines.speaker: James
lines.text: APAT has confirmed participation in joint monitoring.

lines.speaker: James
lines.text: We can also support the village technician training program.

lines.speaker: James
lines.text: This project may really be different.

lines.speaker: James
lines.text: APAT can provide technical advice.

lines.speaker: James
lines.text: But joint implementation needs further discussion.

lines.speaker: James
lines.text: There is progress, but it is not complete.

lines.speaker: James
lines.text: Honestly, I'm worried the community foundation is weak.

lines.speaker: James
lines.text: APAT may need to reconsider participation.

lines.speaker: James
lines.text: This is difficult as it stands.

lines.speaker: Sela
lines.text: Land Office speaking.

lines.speaker: Sela
lines.text: Ratu Josefa's signature has been confirmed. We can process the land use consent.

lines.speaker: Sela
lines.text: Land Office speaking.

lines.speaker: Sela
lines.text: We still have not received the village representative's signature.

lines.speaker: Sela
lines.text: Without land use consent, the project cannot proceed.
All reports are finished.
Now it is your turn to state your position.

choices.text: "We will proceed with a model the vanua operates directly. We need everyone's cooperation."
"We will do our best within what is realistically possible."

lines.text: The meeting ends.
Suva's afternoon sunlight enters through the window.
- One year later. Naitamba Island. -
A new water tank stands in the village center. Pipes stretch through the village.
People gather in front of the tank. It is the completion ceremony.
Vikash takes the completion certificate from his briefcase. James hangs the APAT flag.
Sela stamps the completed land consent document.
Mere takes field photos with her camera.
Ratu Josefa steps forward. The village falls quiet.

lines.speaker: Ratu Josefa
lines.text: We waited ten years.

lines.speaker: Ratu Josefa
lines.text: This time... our vanua is the owner.
Ratu pours yaqona into the tanoa. A blessing sevusevu begins.
The yaqona cup goes around. Everyone claps once, drinks in one go, and claps three times.
Lani stands in front of the tank valve.

lines.speaker: Lani
lines.text: ...Ratu, can I turn it?

lines.speaker: Ratu Josefa
lines.text: Io, turn it.
Lani opens the valve. Water flows from the pipe.
Children cheer. Someone begins to clap.
Village youth begin a meke, a traditional dance. Drums echo.

lines.speaker: Mere
lines.text: ...Vinaka. You did well. Truly.

lines.speaker: James
lines.text: This time, it worked properly.

lines.speaker: Vikash
lines.text: I have a good report for the minister.
Wind blows. Palm trees sway.
A sign beside the water tank reads:
"Naitamba Village Water Facility - Managed by the Village Committee"
This time, the vanua is the owner.
- One year later. Naitamba Island. -
Construction is underway. The tank foundation is set, and pipe work is in progress.
Part of the budget was cut. The schedule slipped. But the work did not stop.
Villagers are at the construction site. This time, they are not just watching; they are participating.

lines.speaker: Mere
lines.text: It's not 100 percent. But it is definitely different from ten years ago.

lines.speaker: Mere
lines.text: This is a good result. I mean that.

lines.speaker: Ratu Josefa
lines.text: ...It is not finished yet. But this time, perhaps our vanua can hope.

lines.speaker: Lani
lines.text: When it is completed, our vanua will hold a feast. You have to come.
Wind blows. Palm trees sway.
It is not perfect. But the direction is right.
And you are not alone.
- Six months later. KODA Office. -
Unread emails pile up on the desk.
The project was not approved. The budget was pushed to the following year.
Stakeholders contact you less and less. Meetings are no longer scheduled.
Naitamba Island's old water pipes remain as they were. When the dry season comes, the same problem will repeat.
You open the desk drawer. Your predecessor's memo is still there.

lines.speaker: Memo
lines.text: "Naitamba Island project - good luck. Sorry."
You pick up a pen. You write a new memo.

lines.speaker: Memo
lines.text: "Naitamba Island project - good luck. I'm sorry."
You put the memo into the drawer for the next person.
The same ending as ten years ago. Someone else will come again.
That memo will still be in the drawer then.
- Aid World -
The field of international development cooperation is not on paper.
Trust is not built with a single handshake.
Thank you for playing to the end.
